one_line_takeaway: APD shows that jointly tuning visual+textual prompts while online-distilling from a non-robustly pretrained CLIP teacher beats prior unimodal or bimodal adversarial prompt-tuning defenses (APT-T, APT-V, APT-VL, FAP-VL) on clean accuracy/PGD-100/TI-FGSM/AutoAttack robustness across 11 datasets and 2 backbones, without requiring any robustly pretrained backbone.
key_finding: On ViT-B/16, APD achieves average clean accuracy 75.73% and PGD-100 robust accuracy 48.38% across the 11 datasets, beating the strongest prior baseline FAP-VL's PGD-100 robustness (45.25%) by 3.13 points; on ViT-L/14, APD reaches 81.58% clean / 73.53% PGD-100 robust accuracy, beating FAP-VL (69.95%) by 3.58 points. Under black-box TI-FGSM, APD outperforms the best baseline by 3.47% (ViT-B/16) and 4.04% (ViT-L/14) on average. Under AutoAttack (ViT-B/16), APD beats FAP-VL by 3.42% on average, winning on 10/11 datasets (losing only on StanfordCars). Under domain shift (ImageNet -> ImageNet-A/R/Sketch/V2, PGD-100), APD averages 18.68% robust accuracy on ViT-B/16 and 43.18% on ViT-L/14, versus FAP-VL's best comparable average, beating it by 2.6 points on ViT-L/14. The paper's own modality ablation (Table 3) shows unimodal textual defense is largely ineffective (APT-T average PGD robustness 3.86%, APD-T 4.11%) while unimodal visual defense is far stronger (APT-V 39.72%, APD-V 42.33%), and bimodal defense is best (APT-VL 43.78%, APD-VL/APD 48.38%) -- i.e., bimodal APD improves 44.27% over its own unimodal-textual variant and 6.05% over its own unimodal-visual variant. The online-vs-offline ablation shows APD-Offline (teacher frozen after pretraining) already beats APT-VL and FAP-VL (46.12% robust acc vs 43.78%/45.25%), but full online APD (teacher continuously updated with feedback from the student) is best at 48.38%, isolating the online-feedback mechanism as a distinct contributor beyond just distillation itself. A hyperparameter sweep on beta shows robust accuracy peaks at beta=0.2 and both clean and robust accuracy degrade for beta>0.4, because the teacher over-weights student feedback and its soft labels become less reliable natural-accuracy signals.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is Adversarial Prompt Distillation (APD), a bimodal (visual + textual) prompt-tuning defense for CLIP-style vision-language models that jointly optimizes learnable prompts on both the image encoder and text encoder while performing ONLINE knowledge distillation between a 'teacher' CLIP copy (trained on clean/natural images) and a 'student' CLIP copy (trained on on-the-fly PGD adversarial images). This differs from prior Adversarial Prompt Tuning (APT) work in two respects that the authors explicitly claim as novel: (1) it is bimodal rather than single-modality (prior AdvPT/APT tune only textual prompts), and (2) it requires no robustly pre-trained backbone for either the teacher or student -- both are standard, non-robust pre-trained CLIP checkpoints, unlike prior adversarial-distillation methods (ARD, AKD, RSLAD, IAD, PeerAiD) which all assume a robust teacher. The teacher is optimized with a combined cross-entropy (clean labels) + KL-divergence (feedback from the student's adversarial outputs) loss, and the student is optimized to match the teacher's soft labels on adversarial inputs via KL divergence only -- a bi-level min-max formulation (PGD inner maximization, joint teacher/student outer minimization), given as Algorithm 1 in the paper.
threat_model: White-box, black-box (transfer), and AutoAttack image-perturbation adversaries against a downstream CLIP-based image classifier. Attacks are L_infinity-bounded pixel perturbations only (no text-based or bimodal attacks are used for evaluation, though the Related Work section surveys text-based and bimodal attacks such as Co-Attack, SGA, SA-Attack, Ot-Attack as prior work, not as attacks used in this paper's own experiments). Training-time perturbation budget epsilon=1/255 with PGD (2 steps, step size alpha=1/255); evaluation uses the same epsilon but 100-step PGD (white-box), 100-step TI-FGSM as a transfer/black-box attack, and AutoAttack (epsilon=1/255) as the strongest attack. The defender assumes access to only a standard, non-robustly pre-trained CLIP backbone (no adversarially pre-trained backbone required), which the paper frames as a more realistic/challenging deployment setting than prior work.
limitations: The paper's own stated/implied limitations: (1) robustness only evaluated against L_infinity image-space attacks (PGD, TI-FGSM, AutoAttack) at a fairly small perturbation budget (epsilon=1/255 used for both training and most evaluation, with only a narrow sweep up to 4/255 in the ablation, where robustness drops sharply); no text-based or bimodal (image+text) adversarial attacks are evaluated despite being surveyed in Related Work. (2) The method underperforms APT-T in clean accuracy on ViT-L/14 (81.58% vs 82.89%, a 1.31-point gap) despite far superior robustness (73.53% vs 38.03%), an explicit robustness/accuracy trade-off the authors acknowledge. (3) On StanfordCars, APD is beaten by baselines in both black-box (ViT-B/16) and AutoAttack settings -- an exception to its otherwise consistent superiority, noted but not explained. (4) At the largest tested perturbation length (prompt length 32) and larger perturbation budgets, robustness degrades, which the authors attribute to underfitting from limited few-shot training data. (5) Evaluation is limited to CLIP-family VLMs with ViT-B/16 and ViT-L/14 vision backbones; generalization to other VLM architectures (e.g., BLIP, generative VLMs) is not tested. (6) The teacher and student in APD are both non-robust pretrained CLIP; the paper does not compare against a setting where the teacher is a robustly pretrained model (that comparison is deliberately excluded from scope, since the point is to avoid needing a robust backbone), so the extent to which APD's gains would compound with an available robust teacher is unaddressed.

quantitative_results: ViT-B/16 average clean accuracy: APD 75.73% vs FAP-VL 75.34%, APT-VL 70.98%, APT-V 73.62%, APT-T 74.42%
ViT-B/16 average PGD-100 robust accuracy: APD 48.38% vs FAP-VL 45.25%, APT-VL 43.78%, APT-V 39.72%, APT-T 3.86%
ViT-L/14 average clean accuracy: APD 81.58% vs APT-T 82.89% (APD is 1.31 points lower), FAP-VL 78.66%
ViT-L/14 average PGD-100 robust accuracy: APD 73.53% vs APT-T 38.03%, FAP-VL 69.95% (APD +3.58 over FAP-VL)
Black-box TI-FGSM average: APD beats best baseline by 3.47% (ViT-B/16) and 4.04% (ViT-L/14)
AutoAttack (ViT-B/16): APD beats FAP-VL by 3.42% average, wins 10/11 datasets (loses on StanfordCars)
Domain shift average robustness: ViT-B/16 APD 18.68%; ViT-L/14 APD 43.18% vs FAP-VL 2.6 points lower

Modality ablation (Table 3, ViT-B/16 avg PGD): APT-T 3.86% -> APD-T 4.11%; APT-V 39.72% -> APD-V 42.33%; APT-VL 43.78% -> APD-VL(=APD) 48.38%; bimodal APD-VL beats unimodal APD-T by 44.27 points and APD-V by 6.05 points
Online vs offline (Fig.7, ViT-B/16 avg): clean/robust -- APT-VL 70.98/43.78, FAP-VL 75.34/45.25, APD-Offline 75.70/46.12, APD(online) 75.73/48.38
Beta sweep: robust accuracy peaks at beta=0.2; both clean and robust accuracy decline for beta>0.4
Prompt depth sweep (ViT-B/16, avg PGD-100): depth=3 -> 37.79%, depth=6 -> 44.73%, depth=9 -> 46.61%, depth=12 -> 48.38% (peak)
baselines_compared: APT-T (Zhang et al. AdvPT / Li et al. APT, textual-only prompt defense)
APT-V (paper's own adversarial VPT-based visual-only prompt baseline)
APT-VL (paper's own bimodal APT baseline combining deep visual+text prompts)
FAP-VL (Zhou et al. 2024, Few-shot Adversarial Prompt learning, strongest prior bimodal baseline)
